rationale: This is the classic, simplest example of the Polonovski reaction, showing the conversion of trimethylamine N-oxide to dimethylvinylamine. The N-oxide is activated by acetic anhydride, forming an O-acetylated intermediate. A base (often acetate formed in situ) then abstracts a proton from one of the methyl groups, leading to an E2-like elimination to form the enamine product and acetic acid. This example clearly demonstrates the core transformation: activation of a tertiary amine N-oxide followed by deprotonation at the alpha-carbon to form an iminium ion equivalent, which in this case eliminates to an enamine.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic structures to avoid overlap with other named reactions involving aromatic rings, complex alkaloids, or specific functional groups. The transformation from a tertiary amine N-oxide to an enamine via acylation is highly characteristic of the Polonovski reaction and distinct from other amine oxidations (e.g., Cope elimination, which is thermal and requires a beta-hydrogen) or rearrangements.
